front: What are the three original V's that define Big Data?
back: Volume, Velocity, and Variety. These represent the massive scale of data (Volume), the speed at which it is generated and processed (Velocity), and the different types of data, both structured and unstructured (Variety).

front: What is the core definition of Big Data?
back: Big Data refers to extremely large and complex datasets that are beyond the ability of traditional data processing software to capture, manage, and process within a tolerable elapsed time.

front: Name two additional V's often added to the definition of Big Data.
back: Veracity and Value. Veracity refers to the quality, accuracy, and trustworthiness of the data. Value refers to the ability to turn data into useful insights and business benefits.

front: What is the primary technological shift that enabled the practical use of Big Data?
back: The shift from centralized, scale-up systems to distributed, parallel processing on clusters of commodity hardware (scale-out), popularized by technologies like Hadoop.

front: How does Big Data analytics differ from traditional Business Intelligence (BI)?
back: Traditional BI primarily analyzes structured, historical data for descriptive reporting. Big Data analytics can process vast volumes of multi-structured data in near real-time to enable predictive and prescriptive analytics, uncovering new insights rather than just monitoring past performance.
